steps.title: Recognize the parallel structure
steps.content: Both Hayden's and Malik's poems are presented as examples of ekphrastic poetry — each explores a visual artwork. The transition must signal that Malik's poem does the same thing as Hayden's.

steps.title: Select the transition for parallel examples
steps.content:
The logical relationship is addition via parallel example: a second case mirrors the first.
  • Choice A — ‘nevertheless,’: signals a concession despite prior conditions. Malik's poem does not stand in contrast to Hayden's; it does the same thing. ✗
  • Choice B — ‘conversely,’: signals an opposite situation. Both poems take paintings as subjects, so there is no opposition here. ✗
  • Choice C — ‘likewise,’: signals that the second case does the same as the first — "in the same way." Malik's poem likewise takes a painting as its subject. ✓
  • Choice D — ‘hence,’: signals a conclusion or result drawn from prior reasoning. Malik's poem is not a consequence of Hayden's poem. ✗
That confirms C is correct.
